title: Dog Drooling and Foaming at the Mouth: Emergency Guide and Causes
meta_desc: Is your dog drooling excessively or foaming? Learn about hypersalivation, when it is an emergency, and why you should act quickly for your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden foaming or excessive drooling in dogs is a high-priority emergency often caused by toxins, heatstroke, or bloat, and requires immediate veterinary attention.

What causes sudden hypersalivation and foaming at the mouth in dogs?

Excessive drooling, medically known as ptyalism, and foaming at the mouth occur when a dog is unable to swallow saliva or is producing it at an abnormal rate. While some breeds naturally drool, a sudden onset of thick foam or puddles of saliva often indicates a physical or neurological reaction to an underlying health issue.

Is my dog foaming at the mouth a medical emergency?

  • Sudden and excessive foaming or drooling is considered a high-priority medical emergency.
  • Common causes include toxin ingestion (such as chocolate, pesticides, or poisonous toads), heatstroke, gastric torsion (bloat), or severe allergic reactions.
  • Seek immediate veterinary care if your dog also experiences tremors, difficulty breathing, or lethargy.

How does taking a photo of my dog's drool help with veterinary triage?

  • If your dog is stable enough to move, take a quick photo or video of the drooling or foam for the veterinary team.
  • Different consistencies, such as thick white foam versus clear liquid, help vets differentiate between nausea, chemical burns, or neurological distress.
  • Providing visual evidence allows for faster diagnosis and treatment upon your arrival at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive drooling and foaming at the mouth in dogs can be caused by a variety of factors, some of which require immediate veterinary attention. Potential causes include oral foreign bodies, neoplasms, injuries, mucosal defects, infectious diseases such as rabies or the nervous form of distemper, convulsive disorders, motion sickness, fear, nervousness, excitement, reluctance to swallow due to esophageal irritation or obstruction, sublingual lesions, tonsillitis, administration of medicine, conformational defects such as heavy, pendulous lower lips, metabolic disorders like hepatic encephalopathy or uremia, and salivary gland issues such as abscesses. Furthermore, ingestion of certain topical agents like 5-Fluorouracil and Calcipotriene can be fatal even at low doses and can present with salivation. The possibility of rabies should always be eliminated first, and treatment should focus on addressing the underlying cause, whether local or systemic.

Chapter: Neurology, Gastroenterology, Infectious Disease, Dentistry, Emergency, Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 368)

faqs.question: What are common causes of foaming at the mouth?
faqs.answer: Common causes include ingestion of toxic substances, dental infections, intense anxiety, heat exhaustion, or gastrointestinal issues like bloat.

faqs.question: Should I try to wash my dog's mouth out?
faqs.answer: Only do so if you are certain they licked a mild irritant like soap. If you suspect a toxin or if your dog is disoriented, do not put your hands near their mouth as they may bite reflexively.

faqs.question: Can motion sickness cause this?
faqs.answer: Yes, many dogs experience hypersalivation and mild foaming due to nausea or anxiety during car rides, though this should subside once the movement stops.
